coredata.dc:title: N,S co-doped carbon dots based fluorescent “on-off-on” sensor for determination of ascorbic acid in common fruits
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Xueli
Zhang, Wengang
Han, Yong
Chen, Xiumei
Zhu, Li
Tang, Wenzhi
Wang, Jianlong
Yue, Tianli
Li, Zhonghong
coredata.dc:description: A switchable sensor based on N and S co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) was developed to determine ascorbic acid (AA) content in common fruits via fluorescence quenching of N,S-CDs by Fe3+ (off) and recovery by AA (on). TEM showed that the particle size was 2.35–7.95 nm with an average diameter of 5.12 nm. XPS and FTIR confirmed the presence of –OH, –COOH and –NH2 on N,S-CDs surface. UV–vis and FTIR revealed that the coordination of –COOH and –NH2 on N,S-CDs surface with Fe3+ accounted for FL quenching, and the release of –NH2 from coordination complexes due to the reduction of Fe3+ by AA led to partly FL recovery. The sensor allowed detecting AA in the linear range of 10–200 μmol/L with a LOD of 4.69 μmol/L. AA contents in common fruits detected by the sensor were in good agreement with the reference method (P > 0.05), indicating a practical fluorescent sensor for AA detection in common fruits.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ascorbic acid
Fluorescence detection
Switchable sensor
Nanosensor
Fruits

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence assay of AA in fruit samples
Design strategy of N,S-CDs/Fe3+chemosensor for AA
Detection of AA in fruit samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of N,S-CDs; Inset: The particle size distribution of N,S-CDs; (B) FTIR spectra of N,S-CDs, N,S-CDs/Fe3+, N,S-CDs/Fe3+ + AA; (C) XPS survey spectrum of N,S-CDs; (D) UV–vis absorption (black line), excitation (red line), and emission (blue line) spectra of N,S-CDs (the insets of (a) and (b) are before and after excited with 365 nm UV lamp). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(A) FL intensity of CDs at different pH; (B) Response of final concentration of Fe3+ to Effq and Effr; (C) Incubation time versus FL intensity.

(A) FL emission spectra of N,S-CDs in presence of 0.5 mmol/L Fe3+ upon addition of AA at different concentrations; (B) The plot of the fluorescence recovery factor (F/F0) versus concentrations of AA. (Inset: a linear region).
FL intensity response of N,S-CDs/Fe3+ to 200 μmol/L AA and interfering species (5-fold concentration of AA, except ions are 100-fold concentration of AA) in the absence of AA.
Schematic illustration of the N,S-CDs/Fe3+system for detection of AA.
Detected AA contents in various Fruits.

head.ce:title: N,S co-doped carbon dots based fluorescent “on-off-on” sensor for determination of ascorbic acid in common fruits

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University, Yangling, Shaanxi 712100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Quality & Safety Risk Assessment for Agro-products (YangLing), Ministry of Agriculture, Yangling, Shaanxi 712100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Quality & Safety Risk Assessment for Agro-products (YangLing)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Engineering Research Center of Agriculture Integration Test (Yangling), Yangling, Shaanxi 712100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Research Center of Agriculture Integration Test (Yangling)

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University, Yangling, Shaanxi 712100, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A switchable sensor based on N and S co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) was developed to determine ascorbic acid (AA) content in common fruits via fluorescence quenching of N,S-CDs by Fe3+ (off) and recovery by AA (on). TEM showed that the particle size was 2.35–7.95 nm with an average diameter of 5.12 nm. XPS and FTIR confirmed the presence of –OH, –COOH and –NH2 on N,S-CDs surface. UV–vis and FTIR revealed that the coordination of –COOH and –NH2 on N,S-CDs surface with Fe3+ accounted for FL quenching, and the release of –NH2 from coordination complexes due to the reduction of Fe3+ by AA led to partly FL recovery. The sensor allowed detecting AA in the linear range of 10–200 μmol/L with a LOD of 4.69 μmol/L. AA contents in common fruits detected by the sensor were in good agreement with the reference method (P > 0.05), indicating a practical fluorescent sensor for AA detection in common fruits.

ce:section.ce:para: Ascorbic acid (AA or Vitamin C) is one of the most important water-soluble vitamins. AA, as an indispensable nutrient, widely exists in fresh fruits. The content of AA is considered as an important indicator for evaluating the freshness and nutritional quality of fruits (Ahvenainen, 1996). AA is a well-known micronutrient and potent antioxidant which plays vital roles inhuman body growth, metabolism and development process. For example, AA can prevent free radical induced diseases like cancer, cardiovascular disease and Parkinson’s disease. Besides, AA is essential to promote the formation of antibody and iron absorption (O'Connell, Gormally, Pravda, & Guilbault, 2001), and stabilize vitamin E and folic acid (Chen et al., 2008). AA is involved in the synthesis of carnitine, catecholamine and myelin as an enzymatic cofactor (Liu, Hu, & Su, 2012). Nowadays, AA has been increasingly used in food additives, medicines to improve food quality and human health (André, Castanheira, Cruz, Paseiro, & Sanches-Silva, 2010). However, AA cannot be synthesized by human body and its deficiency may cause several diseases including common cold, scurvy, mental illness, infertility, and cancer (Moghadam, Dadfarnia, Shabani, & Shahbazikhah, 2011). Dietary intake of fresh fruits is a simple way to supply AA for maintaining human health. Therefore, determination of AA in fresh fruits is of great importance for diet guidance and quality monitoring of fruits. To date, several methods have been developed for sensitive and specific determination of AA, including chromatography (Gokmen, Kahraman, Demir, & Acar, 2000; Silva, 2005), capillary electrophoresis (CE) (Tang & Wu, 2005), titrimetry (Arya, Mahajan, & Jain, 2000), electrochemistry (EC) (Pardakhty, Ahmadzadeh, Avazpour, & Gupta, 2016), spectrophotometry (Guclu, Sozgen, Tutem, Ozyurek, & Apak, 2005) and enzymology (Kelly & Latzko, 1980). Nevertheless, these techniques are mostly time-consuming, expensive, specific instrumentations and skilled personnel needed. The development of simple, time-saving and cost-effective methods for the detection of AA in fruits are indispensable in view of complicated sample pretreatment procedures along with the use of biochemical reagents.

Fluorescence (FL) analytical techniques are suitable for rapid detection of various organic/inorganic analytes due to their simplicity, high sensitivity, easy operation and low cost. FL of these methods usually origins from luminescent platforms such as quantum dots (QDs), metallic nanoclusters (e.g. Au/Ag/Cu nanoclusters), luminescent nanoparticles or organic fluorescent dyes. Compared to organic chromophores and nanoclusters, QDs are widely explored owing to their highly luminous intensity and stability as well as excellent spectral characteristics. However, conventional QDs, compounds of III-V and II-VI elements, are involved in complicated and expensive synthesis, poor biocompatibility. In addition, they are potential threats to food and environment safety due to the existence of heavy metals (Huang et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2017). Applications of eco-friendly QDs for FL sensing are urgently essential and promising. Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), one of the 0D carbonic nanomaterials, have attracted increasing research interests in many fields including bio-imaging (Chen, Liu, Li, Xu, & Qiao, 2017), chemo- and bio-sensing (Godavarthi et al., 2017; Simoes, Leitao, & Esteves da Silva, 2017), drug delivery, photo-catalysis and optronics (Tang et al., 2014) due to their fascinating advantages of easy synthesis with raw carbon sources, desirable biocompatibility, low toxicity, low cost, tunable FL emission and high resistance to photo-bleaching (Lim, Shen, & Gao, 2015). However, the CDs mostly used for FL sensors suffer from relatively low fluorescence quantum yields (FLQY) without surface modification/passivation by polymeric materials or doping with heteroatom. Heteroatom doping (e.g. N, S, B, Si and P) is a simple and efficient approach to improve FL efficiency by improving their optical and electronic properties and to improve affinity of CDs to analytes by providing abundant functional groups/active sites (Qian et al., 2014; Yu, Nagelli, Du, & Dai, 2010). Among these doped CDs, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs are remarkably attractive with high FLQY (Li, Yu, & You, 2015). Thus heteroatom doped CDs may be a superior candidate for applications in food analysis.

Due to the desirable optical and chemical properties of CDs, utilizing CDs as a signal source to develop optical nanosensors emerges as a research hotpot. The current CDs based sensing platforms are constructed upon fluorescence “on-off” model. The FL could be directly quenched by target analytes to give a concentration dependent response. However, the direct quenching analysis is susceptible to environmental stimulus which may debase the analytical performances of a method. Incorporation CDs with FL quenchers (e.g. Hg2+, Fe3+, MnO4 -, MnO2, Cu2+ and Cr2O7 2-) in presence of analytes could develop novel “on-off-on” switchable sensors (Hou et al., 2016), in which the original FL of CDs is firstly quenched by quenchers and then recovered by analytes due to the stronger affinity or reactivity of analytes toward quenchers (Fong, Chin, & Ng, 2016). These indirect fluorescent sensors with higher analytical sensitivity than the “on-off” sensors have been successfully used in detection of drugs, biomolecules, food contaminants and environmental pollutants. Among the quenchers used in the “on-off-on” sensors, Fe3+ is particularly considered due to its nontoxicity compared to heavy metal involved fluorescent sensors (Gong et al., 2017; Zheng et al., 2013) and its high photo-induced electron transfer (PET) quenching efficiency in CDs/Fe3+ systems. Thus it is inspired that combining the highly luminous N,S-CDs with Fe3+ could develop a novel selective and sensitive FL switchable sensor for detecting AA in fresh fruits.

Herein, we present a simple and cost-effective FL switchable sensor for ascorbic acid (AA) detection using N,S-CDs/Fe3+ system (Scheme 1 ). The N,S-CDs prepared using citric acid and thiourea as precursors could be efficiently quenched by Fe3+. When introduced AA into the sensing system, Fe3+ was reduced to Fe2+ along with the FL recovery of N,S-CDs. Based on the phenomenon, a FL switchable sensor was developed to selectively detect AA. The impact of pH of detection system, final concentration of Fe3+, and incubation time (FL quenching and recovery) were investigated. The sensor was feasible to detect AA in real fruits with consistent accuracy compared to the reference fluorescence method.

All chemicals were of analytical reagent grade and were used without further purification. Citric acid (CA) was purchased from Tianjin Bodi Chemical Industry Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Thiourea, acetic acid, sodium acetate and oxalic acid (OA) were obtained from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory (Chengdu, China). Fe(NO3)3·9H2O and HNO3 were purchased from Xilong Chemical Industry Co. Ltd. (Guangdong, China). Ascorbic acid (AA), sucrose (Suc), glucose (Glu), potassium chloride, calcium chloride anhydrous and sodium chloride were purchased from Guangdong Chemical Regent Engineering-technological Research and Development Center (Guangdong, China). Glycine (Gly) was obtained from Beijing Solabio Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Glutathione (GSH), l-malic acid (MA), d-fructose (Fru), l-(+)-tartaric acid (TA) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). The fruit samples were obtained from a local supermarket. Deionized water (DW) was used throughout the experimental procedures.

N,S-CDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method according to literature with certain modifications (Wang, Lu, Hou, Liu, & Zhang, 2016). Briefly, 0.2 g citric acid and 0.4 g thiourea were firstly dissolved into 5 mL DW to form a clear solution under ultrasound. Then the resulting solution was transferred into a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene-equipped Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave, and was hydrothermally reacted at 200 °C for 2 h. The dark yellow product was dialyzed against water using a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight 1000 Da, Beijing Solabio Technology Co., Ltd) for 2 days. Finally, the obtained CDs suspension was filtered through a 0.22 μm polyether sulfone (PES) membrane and stored at 4 °C for further use.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a Vetex70 FTIR Spectroscope (Bruker Corp, Germany) with a scanning speed of 4 cm−1. Morphologies and microstructures were characterized by JEM-2100 (JEOL, Japan) transmission electron microscopy (TEM). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured by a K-Alpha XPS (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA.) for analysis chemical information of the N,S–CDs. UV–vis spectra were collected on a Shimadzu UV–vis 2550 Spectrophotometer (Japan). Fluorescence spectra were recorded using a 10 mm path length quartz cell at room temperature from 300 to 600 nm using a Perkin-Elmer LS-55 Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Maryland, USA). The slit widths of excitation and emission were both 10 nm.

In a typical assay, 200 μL of N,S-CDs solution (8 μg/mL) and 0.5 mmol/L of ferric nitrate solution were successively added into 1 mL acetate buffer (pH = 6, 10 mmol/L) to incubate at room temperature for 3 min. Thereafter, different concentrations of AA standard solutions were added into the mixtures (N,S-CDs/Fe3+) and then were diluted to 4 mL. After incubating at room temperature for 14 min, the FL spectra were measured at excitation wavelength of 360 nm and emission wavelength of 440 nm. All samples for fluorescence measurements were performed in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence assay of AA in fruit samples
ce:section.ce:para: Six different fruits (kiwi, tomato, citrus, pear, grape and apple) with different AA levels were chosen as the representative samples for analytical performance evaluations of the proposed method. The extraction of AA from each fruit sample was carried out according to the literature (Rao et al., 2016). Briefly, the edible part of the fruit was randomly taken and accurately weighed, then mixed with 50 mL 0.2 g/L oxalic acid followed by mashing and centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 20 min in a refrigerated centrifuge at 4 °C. The extract was filtered through a 0.45 μm PES membrane and was then diluted with 0.2 g/L oxalic acid. The samples of kiwi, tomato, citrus, pear and apple were respectively diluted to 10, 4, 4, 1, and 1 times. Detection procedures of AA in these fruit samples were the same as in the standard samples. The analysis data (n = 3) were collected and subjected to statistical analyses. The basic statistics were calculated using Minitab 16.2.3 software (Minitab Inc., USA). A student’s test was applied for difference significance tests (P > 0.05) between different groups.

Morphology, surface functional groups and optical properties of the as-prepared N,S-CDs were investigated. The TEM image of N,S-CDs was shown in Fig. 1 A. Fig. 1A showed that the N,S-CDs were quasi-spherical dots. The inset of Fig. 1A showed that N,S-CDs presented a size distribution range of 2.35–7.95 nm with an average diameter of 5.12 nm, which was acquired by statistical analysis of approximately 100 particles using the Nanomeasure software. FTIR spectrum of the N,S-CDs was shown in Fig. 1B. The peaks at ∼3440 and 2823 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibration of the C–OH and –CH2. A broad O–H band indicates multiple structures of hydroxyl groups on the N,S-CDs surface, endow higher polarity and hydrophilicity (Yang, Zhao, Zhang, Li, & Zhu, 2015). The peaks at ∼2069 and ∼1590 cm−1 were assigned to NH in-plane stretching and NH bending vibration. The peaks at ∼ 1636 and 1400 cm−1 revealed the presence of CO/NH, –COOH. The peak at 1175 cm−1 was related to the stretching vibration of CN and/or CS bond (Hu, et al., 2017). The presence of COC and CO bonds could be evidenced by the characteristic peak at 1005 cm−1. The element composition and valence state of N,S-CDs were confirmed by XPS, and the XPS survey spectrum was shown in Fig. 1C. Four main peaks at 285.0, 532.0, 400.0, and 163.0 eV represent C1s, O1s, N1s, and S2p XPS characteristic peaks. The high-resolution spectra of the four elements were shown in Fig. S1. The spectrum of C1s contained three peaks, indicating CO/CN (288.4 eV) (Li et al., 2015), sp3 hybridized carbon atoms in CS/CN/CO (286.1 eV) and sp2 hybridized carbon atoms in CC/CC (284.4 eV). The O1s XPS spectrum displayed two peaks at ∼531.6 and 532.6 eV, which could be assigned to CO and COH/COC groups. The presence of pyrrolic N and graphite N atoms was confirmed by two peaks at 399.7 and 400.6 eV in the N1s spectrum (Miao et al., 2017). The S2p XPS spectrum presented two peaks at162.2 and 163.3 eV, corresponding to S2p3/2 and S2p1/2 spectra of the CSC covalent bond in thiophene S (Hu et al., 2017). FTIR and XPS analysis verified that N and S atoms were successfully doped into the CDs and the as-prepared N,S-CDs were rich in –COOH, –OH and –NH2 groups which were advantageous for the dispersion of N,S-CDs in aqueous solution. The abundant hydrophilic groups on the surface of N,S-CDs could provide abundant action sites for binding of Fe3+ to N,S-CDs. In addition, these functional groups can generate defects on the surface of N,S-CDs which served as the excitation energy traps, resulting in excellent FL properties (Lai et al., 2013).

The UV–vis and FL spectra of the N,S-CDs were shown in Fig. 1D. As seen from Fig. 1D, the UV–vis spectrum of N,S-CDs showed a weak peak at ∼225 nm, which might be originated from π-π∗ transitions of aromatic CC sp2 domains and thus nearly no FL could be observed (Ye et al., 2017). The absorption peak centered at ∼360 nm could be ascribed to n-π∗ transition of CO or COH on the surface of N,S-CDs, which corresponded to the optimum FL excitation peak of 360 nm. The FL spectra of the N,S-CDs in Fig. 1D revealed that the optimal excitation and emission wavelength of N,S-CDs was 360 nm and 440 nm. A large Stokes’ shift of 80 nm between the absorption and excitation spectra of N,S-CDs indicated a great potential for analytical applications. From the insets of Fig. 1D, the as-prepared N,S-CDs were well-dispersed in water (Fig. 1D a) and exhibited strong blue fluorescence when irradiated with 365 nm UV light (Fig. 1D b), demonstrating that the N,S-CDs showed excellent dispersion and luminescence performances. Fig. S2 showed that the emission peaks of the N,S-CDs at 440 nm was a typically excitation-independent emission when excitation wavelengths changed from 300 to 400 nm with a 20 nm increment, which might reduce the interference of autofluorescence during detection (Guo, Wang, Shao, & Jiang, 2013). Moreover, the resultant N,S-CDs presented satisfied luminous stability for ∼30 d without obvious FL intensity loss, indicating that the N,S-CDs had excellent stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design strategy of N,S-CDs/Fe3+chemosensor for AA
ce:section.ce:para: “Turn-off” The impact of Fe3+ and AA on the FL intensity of N,S-CDs was presented in Fig. S3. As shown in Fig. S3, N,S-CDs exhibited high FL intensity at 440 nm and the FL was efficiently decreased in presence of 0.5 mmol/L Fe3+. FL quenching of CDs by micromolecule organic/inorganic analytes is mostly energy transfer (ET) or electron transfer (Et). The UV-vis absorption spectrum of Fe3+ and the emission spectrum of the N,S-CDs were shown in Fig. S4. There was no obvious spectral overlap between Fe3+ and N,S-CDs, thus the ET process unlikely occurred. The UV–vis and FTIR spectra were used to confirm the quenching mechanism. The UV–vis spectra of N,S-CDs, N,S-CDs/Fe3+ and Fe3+ were shown in Fig. S5. The UV–vis spectra of N,S-CDs were obviously changed upon the addition of Fe3+ compared to that of N,S-CDs or Fe3+, confirming the formation of complex between N,S-CDs and Fe3+. FTIR spectrum of N,S-CDs/Fe3+ (refer to Fig. 1B) showed that the peak at 1400 cm−1 of N,S-CDs disappeared and was split into two weak peaks at 1397 and 1386 cm−1. Meanwhile, the peak at 2065 cm−1 disappeared when Fe3+ was added into N,S-CDs. These changes indicated that Fe3+ coordinated with carboxyl and amine groups on the surface of N,S-CDs. The possible quenching mechanism is that Fe3+ acts as an efficient electron acceptor or hole to initiate a nonradiative PET because iron ion is a kind of paramagnetic transition metal ions with half-filled 3d orbits (Gong et al., 2015). The electrons in the excited stage of N,S-CDs can transfer to the half-filled 3d orbits of Fe3+, leading to significant FL quenching. In addition, N (electronegativity of 3.05) and S (electronegativity of 2.58) atoms are both electron donors. Hence, electron transfer between N,S-CDs and Fe3+ would be facilitated by the introduction of N and S. The previous report (Li et al., 2015) suggested that S doping could effectively tune the electronic local density of N,S-CDs because the valence electrons of S in the third shell tend to lose easily, which would promote the coordination interaction between functional groups on the surface of N,S-CDs and Fe3+. Consequently, the fast electron transfer process and promoted interaction can cause significant FL quenching of N,S-CDs.

“Turn-on” The sensing system could be destroyed and the FL of N,S-CDs was gradually recovered with the increase concentrations of AA introduced into the system (refer to Fig. S3). But the FL intensity at 440 nm did not change when only AA was added to the N,S-CDs solution, indicating that AA did not affect the emission. The results demonstrated that FL recovery of N,S-CDs could be ascribed to the redox reaction between Fe3+ and AA (Eq. (1)). The enediol group of AA is oxidized to an ortho-dione group of dehydroascorbic acid (DHAA) along with the reduction of Fe3+ to Fe2+. Fe2+ showed much lower ability to chelate N,S-CDs and the FL of N,S-CDs can be partly restored. The form of Fe3+ after the introduction of AA into N,S-CDs/Fe3+ was investigated. 1,10-phenanthroline was added into the N,S-CDs/Fe3+ and N,S-CDs/Fe3+ + AA, and the UV–vis spectra were shown in Fig. S6. The N,S-CDs/Fe3++AA in the presence of 1,10-phenanthroline presented an obvious absorption at ∼510 nm compared to N,S-CDs/Fe3+, which can be attributed to the orange-red complex of Fe2+ with 1,10-phenanthroline. The UV–vis spectra further verified the existence of Fe2+ in the system of N,S-CDs /Fe3++AA (S. Chen et al., 2015). When AA was added into N,S-CDs/Fe3+, the peak at 2065 cm−1 was recovered and shifted to 2055 cm−1 while the peaks at 1397 and 1386 cm−1 did not shift, confirming that –NH2 was released and thus the FL emission partly recovered (refer to Fig. 1B). All these results indicated that the N,S-CDs/Fe3+ could develop an effective fluorescence “on–off-on” sensor for detection of AA.

Before investigation of the impact on FL recovery, several tests were carried out to optimize the sensing parameters, including the pH of detection system, concentration of the quencher Fe3+ and the incubation time.

The effect of media pH on FL intensity of N,S-CDs was shown in Fig. 2 A. As seen from Fig. 2A, the FL intensity of N,S-CDs increased rapidly in the pH range of 3.0–5.0, then sequentially increased with a relatively low rate within pH 5.0–6.0, and again increased fast from pH 6.0–8.0. The FL intensity finally leveled to a constant value in the range of 8.0–10.0 (P > 0.05). When the pH increased in the first range of 3.0–5.0, carboxylate groups and amine groups were protonated, leading to negative charged N,S-CDs. The protonation of carboxyl group on the surface of N,S-CDs was slowly taken over by deprotonation process with the increase of pH, resulting in the negatively charged carboxyl groups which can attract the oppositely charged Fe3+. The –NH3 + located on the surface of N,S-CDs tended to increasingly deprotonate from pH 6.0 to 8.0, thus enhanced the electrostatic repulsion among N,S-CDs and increased the FL intensity. The stable FL emission of N,S-CDs in the pH range of 8.0–10.0 might be due to the deprotonation equilibrium of –NH3 + and –COOH. AA is easily oxidized due to the presence of enediol group and lactone group, especially for a base media. The ascorbate anion (AH-), the predominant species of AA at pH > pKa with a low pKa of 4.25, has high reducing ability because the anion can be easily deprotonated to a radical cation at solution pH > 4.25 (Anjali Devi, Salini, Anulekshmi, Praveen, & Sony, 2017). Nevertheless, Fe3+ presented poor stability in alkaline media due to the formation of insoluble ferric hydroxide. When pH changed from 5.0 to 6.0, the positively charge Fe3+ with good stability might has high electrostatic bonding affinity toward N,S-CDs. All the results indicated that pH 6.0 was the optimal pH for the following experiments.

The effect of Fe3+ concentration on FL quenching and recovery were investigated, and the curves were shown in Fig. 2B. As seen from Fig. 2B, the FL quenching efficiency (Effq, Eq. (2)) increased gradually when Fe3+ concentration <0.75 mmol/L, then reached equilibrium where the Effq was almost constant even increasing Fe3+ concentration to 1.25 mmol/L (P > 0.05), indicating that the amount of Fe3+ bonded to N,S-CDs continually increased until reaching saturation. The FL recovery efficiency (Effr, Eq. (3)) slowly decreased in the tested concentration of Fe3+, and the maximum Effr of 34.48% was observed at Fe3+ concentration of 0.5 mmol/L. Appropriate concentration of Fe3+ in N,S-CDs/Fe3+ system was crucial for improving the analytical performances because deficiency or excessive Fe3+ would result in low Effq and Effr, which was not beneficial to sensitive detection of AA (Fong et al., 2016). Therefore, 0.5 mmol/L Fe3+ was chosen for sensing AA.

(2)
Eff
q
( % ) = (
F
0
- F ) /
F
0
(3)
Eff
r
( % ) = (
F
r
- F ) / (
F
0
- F )
where F and F0 represented the FL intensity of N,S-CDs at 440 nm in the presence and absence of Fe3+; Fr is recovered FL intensity of N,S-CDs at 440 nm after adding AA into the sensing system.

The incubation time (quenching and recovery time) was investigated to evaluate the FL sensing response time to AA, and the curves of FL intensity versus incubation time were shown in Fig. 2C. As seen from Fig. 2C, the FL intensity of N,S-CDs gradually decreased after the addition of 0.5 mmol/L Fe3+ and then leveled to a stable value after 3 min (P > 0.05), indicating that adequate functional groups (e.g. –NH2, –OH and –COOH) located on N,S-CDs could quickly interact with Fe3+ to give rise to FL quenching. After adding 1 mmol/L AA into the system of N,S-CDs/Fe3+, the FL intensity of N,S-CDs increased with a relatively high rate in 2 min and then became slower in 2–14 min. The N,S-CDs FL intensity was stable even the reaction time exceed to 14 min (P > 0.05), indicating that 14 min was sufficient for AA to reduce Fe3+ and recover the FL of N,S-CDs. Hence, the FL quenching time of 3 min and recovery time of 14 min were chosen for incubation.

To develop a switchable fluorescent “on-off-on” method for AA sensing, the acetic acid buffer, N,S-CDs (8 μg/mL), 0.5 mmol/L Fe3+ solution were successively mixed and incubated at room temperature for 3 min. And then different concentrations of AA were added into the above system and diluted to a total volume of 4 mL using acetic acid buffer. The FL spectra were scanned after incubating for 14 min, which were shown in Fig. 3 A. As seen from Fig. 3A, the FL intensity of N,S-CDs gradually increased with increasing concentration of AA. The plot of the fluorescence recovery factor F/F0 (F0 and F was the FL intensity of N,S-CDs/Fe3+ sensing system at 440 nm in the absence and presence of AA, respectively) against concentrations of AA was shown in Fig. 3B. A good linear relationship between F/F0 and AA concentration was obtained in the range of 10–200 μmol/L with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9924. The linear equation was expressed as F/F0 = 0.0019[C] + 1.8684 (μmol/L), where C is the concentration of AA. The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 4.69 μmol/L according to Eq. (4).
(4)
LOD = 3 σ / k
where k was the slop of the calibration curve and σ was the standard deviation of bank signals of the N,S-CDs/Fe3+ sensing system (n = 9).

Precisions evaluated by five replicate detection of 20, 80, 200 μmol/L AA standard solutions were 0.98%, 1.36%, 1.02% (RSDs). The comparison of different analytical sensors for AA was presented in Table S1. As shown in Table S1, the LOD of this work was a little bit greater than the conventional QDs FL sensors (Gong et al., 2017). However, the FL switchable sensor in this work was established using highly luminescent nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots as the signal platform and environment friendly Fe3+ as an efficient quencher. It possessed several advantages of quick response, relatively wide sensing range and good operational safety with simple material preparation, which made it promising for food analysis.

Selectivity of the switchable sensor was crucial for determination of AA in real fruits which contained mineral ions, organic acids, carbohydrates and amino acids. Thus, the potentially co-existed interferences (e.g. Glu, Fru, Suc, Gly, CA, MA, TA, OA, GSH, Ca2+, Na+ and K+) were chosen to evaluate the selectivity of this method, and the histograms of FL intensity response [(F/F0) − 1] were shown in Fig. 4 . It was seen from Fig. 4 that AA could effectively recover the FL of N,S-CDs/Fe3+ with the highest [(F/F0) − 1] of 1.23. Fru, Glu, Suc, Gly, OA, Ca2+, Na+ and K+ in detection system had negligible effect on FL response of N,S-CDs/Fe3+ for AA (FL response < 5%). Nevertheless, TA, MA, CA and GSH had weak interference for AA determination in aqueous solution, following the order of TA > CA > MA > GSH. The three reducing organic acids (TA, CA and MA, their chemical formulas were shown in Fig. 4) possessed carboxylic structures and α-hydroxyl which could promote reduction. Hence, TA, CA and MA showed relatively strong and sequential reducing abilities for Fe3+ to generate FL recovery. GSH, a small molecule tripeptide consisted of glutamate, cysteine and glycine, also had multiple binding sites for Fe3+ such as –COOH, –NH2 and –SH, which allowed competing and/or reducing Fe3+ from the surface of N,S-CDs to recover FL. In summary, the developed fluorescent switchable sensor showed acceptable selectivity for AA and could be capable to detect AA with the existence of interference species.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AA in fruit samples
ce:section.ce:para: Practical applications of N,S-CDs/Fe3+ sensor for AA determination in fruits (kiwi, tomato, citrus, pear, grape and apple) were carried out and a reference fluorescence method was used to validate the results. In the reference method, AA is oxidized by activated charcoal into DHAA. DHAA can react with o-phenylenediamine (OPDA) and generate a fluorescent compound (quinoxaline). The detected AA contents and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) were listed in Table 1 . The detected concentrations by the switchable sensor were consistent with those by the reference fluorescence method (P > 0.05), indicating that the proposed switchable sensor presented acceptable precision and accuracy and could be used for practical detection of AA contents in some common fruits except apple. The failure for apple might be ascribed to the relatively low content of AA. In summary, the proposed AA sensor has high selectivity and stability which guarantees its practical applications.

A fluorescence switchable sensor was developed based on nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal method for the detection of AA in common fruits. The N,S-CDs with high fluorescence intensity and stability were obviously quenched by Fe3+ through a possible photo-induced electron transfer mechanism and then effectively recovered by AA based on the redox reaction. The sensor showed rapid fluorescence quenching and recovery response and could detect AA with acceptable selectivity and sensitivity. The switchable sensor could be applied for routine detection of AA in common fruits with acceptable accuracy and precision. This new method, which utilized the highly luminescent N,S-CDs and eco-friendly Fe3+, possessed potential advantages in comparison with chromatography and conventional QDs involved fluorescence methods. The present work provided a novel and practical method for determination of AA in complex fruit matrixes.
This work was supported by the basic work of science and technology of Ministry of Science and Technology of China (2013FY113400), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31371813).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2018.03.032.
